attributes.stem:
A 27-year-old Caucasian woman is referred to the neurology clinic for a follow-up after an acute painful right eye two weeks ago. Her vision has not improved for the last two weeks.

Her clinical examination demonstrates visual acuity 6/6 in the left eye and 6/12 in the right eye, with a positive relative afferent pupillary defect in her right eye.

Her gadolinium-enhanced MRI brain shows ten non-enhancing lesions across the brain's white matter, the juxtacortical and periventricular regions. There is bright enhancement of the right optic nerve, consistent with inflammation. There are no space-occupying lesions, haemorrhages, or signs of ischaemia.

She was commenced on 500mg/day of methylprednisolone for 5 days.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate diagnostic investigation?
attributes.explanation:
The lesions' distribution pattern, i.e. at the white matter, periventricular and juxtacortical regions, should make us highly suspicious of multiple sclerosis (MS). An attack without clinical or radiological evidence of a previous attack is known as a clinically isolated syndrome (CIS). If patients with CIS present with MRI lesions >2, there is about 80% chance of developing into MS.

MS is diagnosed according to the McDonald Criteria (2001) which has undergone major revisions over the years and are now set out as:

  1. ≥2 clinical attacks AND ≥2 lesions with objective clinical evidence

  2. ≥2 clinical attacks AND 1 lesion with objective clinical evidence (as well as clear-cut historical evidence of a previous attack involving a lesion in a distinct anatomical location)

  3. ≥2 clinical attacks AND 1 lesion with objective clinical evidence AND Dissemination in space demonstrated by an additional clinical attack implicating a different CNS site or by MRI

  4. 1 clinical attack AND ≥2 lesions with objective clinical evidence AND Dissemination in time demonstrated by an additional clinical attack or by MRI OR demonstration of CSF-specific oligoclonal bands

  5. 1 clinical attack AND 1 lesion with objective clinical evidence AND lesions that fulfill Dissemination in space AND Dissemination in time OR demonstration of CSF-specific oligoclonal bands

Although previously considered an essential part of the diagnosis of MS, the most recent criteria (2017) no longer require the presence of lesions that are disseminated in time. In CIS patients, the presence of lesions that show dissemination in space and unmatched CSF oligoclonal bands now permits a diagnosis of MS.

In this patient, dissemination in time is not established radiographically (which would show up as Gd-enhancing lesions) or clinically (previous attacks) in this case, so an LP for CSF-specific oligoclonal band (IgG) could be performed. It should be noted that enhancing lesions of the optic nerve (optic neuritis) cannot be used in fulfilling the 2017 McDonald criteria. Oligoclonal bands that are found exclusively in the CSF but not the serum is indicative of a neuroinflammatory disorder, which is present in up to 95% of patients with clinically definite MS.

An MRI spine could be useful in assessing the extent of MS but not necessarily confirming time dissemination. An MRI has already been performed at this current time, showing multiple lesions, but to establish dissemination in time, a second MRI would need to be done at a different time point to identify new or enhancing lesions. Therefore, an MRI spine at this time would not be able to provide a diagnosis.

Nerve conduction studies are usually indicated in peripheral neuropathies instead of CNS neuropathies such as MS.

Any findings in blood immunoglobulin and antibody panels are used compared with CSF findings, instead of being a diagnostic test itself.

A CT brain would not be helpful in giving new information as it is less defined than an MRI brain.
learningPoints: Multiple sclerosis typically occur in young, white females and the most common presentation of multiple sclerosis is optic neuritis.

Multiple sclerosis can be diagnosed based on the presence of lesions demonstrated radiologically during at least two different time points, but also can be diagnosed based o the presence of radiological findings at one point in time and the presence of  CSF-specific oligoclonal band (IgG) on lumbar puncture.
references: de Angelis, F. (2019, February 1). New MS diagnostic criteria in practice. Practical Neurology. https://pn.bmj.com/content/19/1/64?
Evaluation and diagnosis of multiple sclerosis in adults. (2021, October). UpToDate. https://www.uptodate.com/contents/evaluation-and-diagnosis-of-multiple-sclerosis-in-adults?

Thompson AJ, Banwell BL, Barkhof F, Carroll WM, Coetzee T, Comi G, Correale J, Fazekas F, Filippi M, Freedman MS, Fujihara K, Galetta SL, Hartung HP, Kappos L, Lublin FD, Marrie RA, Miller AE, Miller DH, Montalban X, Mowry EM, Sorensen PS, Tintoré M, Traboulsee AL, Trojano M, Uitdehaag BMJ, Vukusic S, Waubant E, Weinshenker BG, Reingold SC, Cohen JA. Diagnosis of multiple sclerosis: 2017 revisions of the McDonald criteria. Lancet Neurol. 2018 Feb;17(2):162-173. doi: 10.1016/S1474-4422(17)30470-2. Epub 2017 Dec 21. PMID: 29275977.
